cdp.name: CDP
cdp.gloss: Customer Data Platform — the unified store of who your customers are.

perso.name: Personalization (1:1)
perso.gloss: Tailoring the message to the individual, not the segment.

genai.name: Generative AI
genai.gloss: Models that produce content — copy, images, replies — from a prompt.

agentic.name: Agentic AI
agentic.gloss: Systems that don't just generate, they decide and act toward a goal.

decis.name: Agentic decisioning
decis.gloss: An agent choosing what to say, when, how often and on which channel — per person.

bandit.name: Bandits / RL
bandit.gloss: The maths that lets a system learn which choice wins by trying and measuring.

cep.name: CEP
cep.gloss: Customer Engagement Platform — where this messaging actually runs.

geo.name: GEO
geo.gloss: Generative Engine Optimization — being visible inside AI answers.

govern.name: Governance
govern.gloss: The controls that make automated decisions safe, explainable and legible.

stories.head: Agentic Commerce Is Reimagining the Brand Search Experience
stories.plain: This piece argues that AI agents are turning the old search box into a conversational, personalized commerce layer — not just a lookup tool. For marketers, it means the funnel from discovery to purchase may increasingly run through agentic systems rather than traditional search or ad clicks, reshaping where and how brands invest.

stories.head: Brand Visibility Scores Vary by AI Model — Framework Scores Are Snapshots, Not Constants
stories.plain: When you measure how well an AI agent can find and trust your brand's data, the result depends heavily on which model is doing the resolving — and models change constantly. This matters for governance: any brand visibility score is a time-stamped reading against one model, not a durable benchmark, forcing marketers to rethink how they track GEO performance.

stories.head: Salesforce's Agentforce struggles expose data quality gap blocking enterprise agentic AI
stories.plain: Slow uptake of Salesforce Agentforce reveals that poor data quality and operational unreadiness are the real blockers to enterprise AI agents — not the technology itself. This matters because it reframes the agentic AI conversation: the problem isn't building the agent, it's having clean enough data and processes for the agent to act on.

stories.head: 5 questions to ask AI vendors before you buy anything
stories.plain: A practical buyer's guide that cuts through vendor hype by focusing on business value proof, data policies, and implementation readiness. For anyone new to MarTech-AI procurement, this is a repeatable framework that changes how purchasing decisions get made — shifting the conversation from features to evidence and risk.

stories.head: 91% of marketers claim an AI search strategy but most lack real execution, new survey finds
stories.plain: A 600-person survey shows a wide gap between claimed AI visibility strategy and actual execution, with 62% saying they're already behind competitors in generative engine optimization. This quantifies how GEO has become a boardroom concern, not just an SEO experiment, and signals budget and tooling shifts ahead.

stories.head: Agentic AI ROI remains elusive: most deployments are siloed with no enterprise-wide adoption yet
stories.plain: An honest assessment finding that virtually all agentic AI deployments stay inside one function or business unit with no publicly known enterprise-wide rollout delivering tangible ROI. This resets expectations for buyers and budget owners who are evaluating whether agentic platforms justify the investment right now.

stories.head: Agent visibility scores are model-dependent — a brand's AI discoverability changes as models change
stories.plain: Argues that brand visibility inside AI agents isn't a fixed score — it shifts as the underlying model changes, making point-in-time audits misleading. This directly affects how marketing teams should budget for and govern GEO efforts, treating model versioning as an ongoing variable rather than a one-time checklist item.

month.skicker: 30-day synthesis · the shape underneath the feed

themes.head: Agentic AI hits real-world friction
body: The promise of autonomous AI agents executing marketing tasks end-to-end is colliding with messy enterprise reality. Salesforce's public struggles are the most visible signal, but the broader pattern is the same: orchestration, permissions, and data quality problems slow deployment far more than the technology itself.

Analysts are now urging marketers to look past the hype and focus on where agents actually generate measurable ROI rather than where they generate press releases. The practical question has shifted from "can we deploy an agent?" to "which narrow workflow justifies the integration cost?"
ev: Agile Brand · ROI from agentic AI

themes.head: AI search rewrites brand visibility rules
body: AI-powered search and agentic commerce are changing which brands get seen — and the mechanism is no longer a single algorithm but a patchwork of models, each with its own training data and citation logic. Research shows 91% of marketers claim an AI search strategy, yet most have only surface-level tactics that pre-date how these models actually surface answers.

The strategic implication is stark: visibility is now model-dependent, meaning a brand that ranks well in one AI surface may be invisible in another. Marketers need to audit how their content and structured data appear across GPT-class, Gemini-class, and retrieval-augmented systems separately — not as a single SEO problem.
ev: Agile Brand · agent visibility is model-dependent
MarTech Series · 91% claim AI search strategy

themes.head: Content as commodity, not advantage
body: Generative AI has commoditised content production so thoroughly that volume and even quality are no longer defensible moats. The insight crystallising across multiple pieces this month is that the advantage now lives in proprietary data, audience relationships, and distribution — not in the content artifact itself.

This forces a hard strategic choice: brands must either invest in first-party data and community that AI cannot replicate, or accept that their content will increasingly be absorbed into AI responses without attribution. The SaaS parallel is instructive — just as software alone stopped being a differentiator, content alone has stopped being one too.
ev: MarTech · content no longer competitive advantage
MarTech · SaaS can no longer compete on software alone
MarTech · look past AI to see where martech is going

themes.head: Data infrastructure as the AI bottleneck
body: Across vendor announcements and analyst commentary, one root cause keeps surfacing for stalled AI projects: the underlying data stack was never built for machine consumption. Siloed systems, inconsistent identifiers, and poor labelling practices mean that most enterprise data cannot feed AI models without expensive remediation.

New entrants like Creatio are explicitly targeting the "Frankenstack" problem — the accumulated patchwork of point solutions that makes clean data pipelines nearly impossible. The buying decision that matters most right now may not be which AI tool to add, but which legacy integrations to retire first.

themes.head: AI adoption erodes marketer skill formation
body: With AI adoption now touching 90% of CX teams, a quieter crisis is emerging: if AI handles execution, junior marketers never develop the judgment that comes from doing the work. The question being raised openly is who builds the next generation of marketing expertise when the repetitive tasks that once trained people are automated away.

This is not a distant HR problem — it affects capability today. Teams that over-automate early are finding they lack the human oversight capacity to catch model errors, evaluate creative quality, or reframe strategy when AI outputs go wrong. Organisations need deliberate skill-building programmes alongside AI deployment, not after it.
ev: MarTech · 5 questions to ask AI vendors

month.takeaway: The month's signal is that AI in marketing has moved from an adoption question to an execution & infrastructure question — and the organisations that will pull ahead are those fixing their data, their skills, and their visibility logic before adding more AI tools.
